title: Salicylate Poisoning: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Salicylate Poisoning
Identify key diagnostic features of Salicylate Poisoning
Apply clinical reasoning to Salicylate Poisoning management
Recognize common pitfalls in Salicylate Poisoning

sections.title: Overview of Salicylate Poisoning
sections.content:
**Salicylate Poisoning**

Mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, tinnitus, alkalinization, hemodialysis indications

**Key Points:**
• Classic: mixed respiratory alkalosis (direct medullary stimulation) + anion gap metabolic acidosis
• Early: respiratory alkalosis dominates; Late: metabolic acidosis predominates
• Symptoms: tinnitus, diaphoresis, nausea, hyperthermia, altered mental status

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Classic: mixed respiratory alkalosis (direct medullary stimulation) + anion gap metabolic acidosis
• Early: respiratory alkalosis dominates; Late: metabolic acidosis predominates

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Symptoms: tinnitus, diaphoresis, nausea, hyperthermia, altered mental status
• Treatment: IV sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine (pH >7.5) — traps ionized salicylate in urine
• Hemodialysis indications: level >100 mg/dL, renal failure, pulmonary edema, seizures, refractory acidosis

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Salicylate Poisoning:**

• Symptoms: tinnitus, diaphoresis, nausea, hyperthermia, altered mental status
• Treatment: IV sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine (pH >7.5) — traps ionized salicylate in urine
• Hemodialysis indications: level >100 mg/dL, renal failure, pulmonary edema, seizures, refractory acidosis

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of Salicylate Poisoning?

options.text: Classic: mixed respiratory alkalosis (direct medullary stimulation) + anion gap metabolic acidosis
options.explanation: Classic: mixed respiratory alkalosis (direct medullary stimulation) + anion gap metabolic acidosis

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

mcqs.question: What is the KEY diagnostic or management consideration for Salicylate Poisoning?

options.text: Hemodialysis indications: level >100 mg/dL, renal failure, pulmonary edema, seizures, refractory acidosis
options.explanation: Hemodialysis indications: level >100 mg/dL, renal failure, pulmonary edema, seizures, refractory acidosis

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Salicylate Poisoning. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Early: respiratory alkalosis dominates; Late: metabolic acidosis predominates
options.explanation: Early: respiratory alkalosis dominates; Late: metabolic acidosis predominates

mcqs.stem: A patient presents 2 hours after ingesting an unknown quantity of acetaminophen. Serum APAP level at 4 hours post-ingestion is 220 mcg/mL. Using the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, what is the appropriate management?

options.text: Start IV N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — the level is above the treatment line
options.explanation: On the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, the treatment line at 4 hours is 150 mcg/mL (FDA line) or 200 mcg/mL (original Rumack-Matthew line). At 220 mcg/mL, the level exceeds both thresholds. IV NAC (150 mg/kg loading, then 50 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h) should be started immediately.

options.text: Recheck level at 8 hours before deciding on treatment
options.explanation: With a clearly supratherapeutic level at 4 hours, treatment should begin immediately. Delaying NAC beyond 8 hours post-ingestion significantly increases risk of hepatotoxicity.

options.text: Administer activated charcoal only — NAC is not indicated within 4 hours of ingestion
options.explanation: Activated charcoal may be beneficial if given within 1-2 hours of ingestion, but it is NOT a substitute for NAC when the level indicates toxicity. NAC should be started as soon as possible.

options.text: Obtain LFTs and treat only if transaminases are elevated
options.explanation: Hepatotoxicity from acetaminophen is delayed (ALT typically rises at 24-72 hours). Normal LFTs at 4 hours do not exclude impending liver injury. The nomogram guides treatment BEFORE liver damage occurs.

references.title: AACT Position Paper: Single-Dose Activated Charcoal
